how_it_works: All three plugins subscribe to `tts.text`/`tts.done`/`interrupt.tts` on the PipelineBus and emit `tts.audio`/`tts.end`/`tts.error` (Cartesia also `tts.word_timestamps`). Cartesia (voice-tts-cartesia/src/index.ts:113) streams each sentence with `continue:true` on a per-turn `context_id` over a shared WebSocketConnection; `finishContext` sends `continue:false,flush:true` (index.ts:148), audio arrives as base64 `data` on chunk frames decoded at index.ts:248-300, and `done:true` triggers `emitEnd` (index.ts:307-312). Cancel sends `{context_id,cancel:true}` and tracks `cancelledContexts` to suppress late frames (index.ts:203,229). Deepgram (voice-tts-deepgram/src/index.ts:125) has no per-message context id, so it carries a single `currentContextId` and an odd-byte `carry` buffer across binary frames (index.ts:227-253); `Flush` ends a turn (acked by `Flushed`→emitEnd at index.ts:202-211) and `Clear` stops on barge-in. Gemini (voice-tts-gemini/src/index.ts:107) is non-streaming: it buffers all `tts.text`, then on `tts.done` calls `generateContent` once with `responseModalities:['AUDIO']`, pushing whole-response audio chunks and a single `tts.end` (index.ts:115-126) — no streaming, no word timestamps, not wired into any pipeline config. Both streaming plugins hardcode `pcm_s16le` egress and validate every payload via `assertAudioPayload`; µ-law is isolated to the telephony serializer edge.

findings.title: Cartesia word-timestamp parser uses the wrong schema — emits ZERO word timestamps against real Cartesia output (silently masked by a test that feeds the wrong shape)
findings.evidence: Code parses `word_timestamps.words` as an array of objects: `const rawWords = Array.isArray(wt["words"]) ? wt["words"] : []; for (const w of rawWords) { if (typeof w === "object" && ... typeof w["word"] === "string" && typeof w["start"] === "number" && typeof w["end"] === "number") { ... words.push({ word: entry.word, startMs: Math.round(entry.start*1000)+chunkOffsetMs ... }) } }`. But the authoritative reference clones in-repo both consume Cartesia `word_timestamps` as THREE PARALLEL ARRAYS, not objects: LiveKit `_clones/.../cartesia/tts.py:485-489` does `zip(word_timestamps["words"], word_timestamps["start"], word_timestamps["end"])`, and Pipecat `_clones/pipecat/.../cartesia/tts.py:680-681` does `_normalize_word_timestamps(msg["word_timestamps"]["words"], msg["word_timestamps"]["start"])`. Real Cartesia `words` entries are bare strings, so each `w` fails `typeof w === "object"` and `words` stays empty — no `tts.word_timestamps` is ever emitted in production. The test at index.test.ts:771-776 feeds `words: [{word,start,end}]` (the wrong, object shape), so it passes green while production is broken.
findings.recommendation: Parse the documented/clone-verified shape: read `word_timestamps.words` (string[]), `word_timestamps.start` (number[]), `word_timestamps.end` (number[]) as parallel arrays and zip them. Rewrite the test fixture to the parallel-array shape that real Cartesia returns. Add a fixture captured from the live API to prevent schema drift.
findings.checklist_ref: §5 TTS-11 (capture word/character timestamps and convert into playback-timed frames); bridge VE-01/TTS acceptance

findings.title: Cartesia adds a cumulative per-chunk offset to word timestamps that neither reference implementation applies — double-counts time, mis-aligning captions/spoken-prefix
findings.evidence: `const priorSamples = this.contextAudioSampleCount.get(contextId) ?? 0; const chunkOffsetMs = Math.floor((priorSamples*1000)/this.sampleRate); ... startMs: Math.round(entry.start*1000) + chunkOffsetMs`. Both reference clones pass Cartesia `start`/`end` straight through with NO added offset: LiveKit `_clones/.../cartesia/tts.py:508-509` `TimedString(text=word, start_time=start, end_time=end)`; Pipecat `_clones/pipecat/.../cartesia/tts.py:683` `add_word_timestamps(processed_timestamps, ...)` with `start` unmodified. Cartesia word timestamps are already absolute to the context generation (the context maintains a single continuous timeline across `continue:true` inputs), so adding `chunkOffsetMs` on top double-counts elapsed audio on every chunk after the first, pushing later words progressively later than the audio they label. The test (index.test.ts:824-826) hard-codes the same wrong offset as the expected value, so it cannot catch this.
findings.recommendation: Remove `chunkOffsetMs` / `contextAudioSampleCount` from the word-timestamp path and emit `start*1000`/`end*1000` directly, matching both verified clones. If a future Cartesia model is confirmed to reset timestamps per chunk, gate the offset behind a verified live capture rather than assuming it.

findings.title: Deepgram single shared `currentContextId` can end the wrong turn on a stale `Flushed`
findings.evidence: Deepgram's socket carries no per-message context id, so `speak` overwrites `this.currentContextId = contextId` (index.ts:129) and the `Flushed` handler ends whatever `currentContextId` currently holds: `const contextId = this.currentContextId; ... if (contextId && this.activeContexts.has(contextId)) { this.activeContexts.delete(contextId); ... this.emitEnd(contextId); }`. The whole plugin's correctness rests on the comment's assumption that the engine 'synthesizes one turn at a time'. If a new `speak(turnB)` is dispatched before turn A's `Flushed` ack returns (e.g. filler `tts.text` for a new context emitted at voice-agent-session.ts:699 while a prior flush is in flight, or any reordering of `tts.text`/`tts.done` across contexts), `currentContextId` is already B, so A's late `Flushed` emits `tts.end` for B and tears down B's audio carry. The `carry` byte buffer (index.ts:62) is likewise per-socket, not per-context, so a turn boundary that lacks a Flushed/Cleared can leak a trailing byte into the next turn.
findings.recommendation: Either assert/document the single-active-turn invariant with a guard (reject/queue a second `speak` while a context is active and unflushed), or track a small FIFO of in-flight context ids and bind `Flushed`/audio to the oldest unacked one. At minimum clear `carry` on every new `speak` to a different contextId.

findings.title: Gemini TTS is non-streaming and unbounded by design but has no guard preventing interactive selection
findings.evidence: `synthesize` buffers all text and calls `client.models.generateContent(...)` once (index.ts:171-189), pushing whole-response audio and a single `tts.end` only after the full promise resolves (index.ts:115-122). There is no streaming, no TTFA, and no word timestamps. The VE-01 bridge requires Gemini be 'marked degraded for interactive VE-01 or excluded from the tracer-bullet default.' Grep confirms `GeminiTTSPlugin` is not referenced by any pipeline/config/script in the tree (only its own package), so the exclusion is satisfied by non-use — but nothing in code prevents wiring it into an interactive/telephony session, where it would violate the streaming-TTS / TTFA invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit capability flag (e.g. `streaming:false`) the session can refuse for interactive modes, or document the exclusion at the plugin boundary so a future config change cannot silently regress TTFA.
findings.checklist_ref: §5 TTS-01/TTS-02 (streaming, not whole-utterance, for interactive); bridge VE-01 risks

findings.title: Cartesia/Deepgram test suites assert provider-frame fixtures of the plugin's own (unverified) shape, weakening regression value for the word-timestamp and protocol paths
findings.evidence: The Cartesia word-timestamp tests fabricate the provider frame in the same wrong object shape the parser expects (`word_timestamps: { words: [{ word: "Hello", start: 0.0, end: 0.4 }, ...] }`) and assert the same cumulative offsets the buggy code computes (`{ word: "Goodbye.", startMs: 100, endMs: 500 }`). Because the fixture is author-invented rather than captured from the live API, the two correctness bugs above pass green. No test in the suite replays a real Cartesia `word_timestamps` parallel-array frame.
findings.recommendation: Add at least one captured-from-live fixture per provider for the timestamp and chunk/done/error frames, and assert against transport-verified bytes. Treat any provider-frame test fixture that is hand-authored as shape-only and not a correctness guarantee.

summary: The streaming spine is solid: Cartesia and Deepgram both stream per-sentence over a shared reconnecting WebSocket, validate every PCM16 payload via assertAudioPayload, isolate µ-law to the telephony edge, implement cancel/Clear on barge-in with a cancelledContexts guard against late frames, and guard the finish-timeout vs done-frame double-emit correctly (mutually-exclusive activeContexts delete). The carry/odd-byte alignment and base64 strictness in both plugins are careful. The real gap is the word-timestamp path in Cartesia: it both (1) parses the wrong schema — objects instead of Cartesia's parallel `words[]/start[]/end[]` arrays, so it emits ZERO timestamps against real output — and (2) adds a cumulative per-chunk offset that neither in-repo reference clone (Pipecat, LiveKit) applies, double-counting time. Both are masked by tests that fabricate the plugin's own wrong frame shape, so TTS-11 ships broken and green. Secondary: Deepgram's single shared currentContextId/carry is correct only under a strict one-turn-at-a-time invariant that is asserted in a comment, not in code, and Gemini is non-streaming with no guard against interactive wiring (currently unwired, so latent). Recommend fixing the Cartesia timestamp schema+offset against a live-captured fixture before claiming TTS-11.
